topic: Later Mughals — Jahangir, Shah Jahan & Aurangzeb

questions.text: The Taj Mahal was built by Shah Jahan in memory of:
options: His wife Mumtaz Mahal (Arjumand Banu Begum)
questions.explanation: The Taj Mahal (1632–1653 CE) in Agra was built by Emperor Shah Jahan as a tomb for his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal (Arjumand Banu Begum), who died in 1631 while giving birth to her 14th child. It is made of white Makrana marble and inlaid with 28 types of precious stones.

questions.text: The English ambassador Thomas Roe visited which Mughal court and obtained trading rights for the East India Company?
questions.explanation: Sir Thomas Roe (ambassador from King James I of England) visited Jahangir's court from 1615–1618 CE. He obtained a firman (imperial order) from Jahangir allowing the English East India Company to trade at Surat. This was the foundation of British commercial presence in India.

questions.text: Aurangzeb reimposed the jizyah (poll tax on non-Muslims) in which year?
questions.explanation: Aurangzeb reimposed jizyah in 1679 CE — 117 years after Akbar had abolished it in 1562 CE. The reimposition was deeply resented and contributed to revolts by Hindus, Rajputs, Marathas, and Jats. It is one of the most tested contrasts in SSC (Akbar abolished jizyah; Aurangzeb reimposed it).

questions.text: Which Mughal emperor was imprisoned by his own son Aurangzeb in the Agra Fort?
questions.explanation: Shah Jahan was imprisoned by his son Aurangzeb in the Agra Fort from 1658 until his death in 1666 — eight years in captivity. He reportedly spent his final years looking at the Taj Mahal across the Yamuna River. His daughter Jahanara Begum voluntarily stayed with him in prison.

questions.text: The first Mughal building to be entirely constructed of marble (introducing the pietra dura technique) was:
questions.explanation: Itimad-ud-Daula's Tomb (Agra, ~1622–1628 CE) — built by Nur Jahan for her father Ghiyas Beg — was the first Mughal structure built entirely of white marble AND the first to use pietra dura (parchin kari — inlaid stonework). The Taj Mahal later perfected these techniques on a grander scale. It is called the 'Baby Taj'.

questions.text: The Guru Tegh Bahadur (9th Sikh Guru) was executed by Aurangzeb in which year?
questions.explanation: Guru Tegh Bahadur, the 9th Sikh Guru, was executed by Aurangzeb in 1675 CE in Delhi (Chandni Chowk) — he refused to convert to Islam. According to Sikh tradition, he gave his life to protect Kashmiri Pandits who had approached him. Gurudwara Sis Ganj Sahib marks the spot. His martyrdom led his son Guru Gobind Singh to found the Khalsa (1699).

questions.text: Nur Jahan's real name before marriage to Jahangir was:
questions.explanation: Nur Jahan's real name was Mehr-un-Nissa. She was the widow of Sher Afghan (a Persian noble). Jahangir married her in 1611 and gave her the title Nur Jahan (Light of the World). Arjumand Banu Begum = Mumtaz Mahal (Shah Jahan's wife); Hamida Banu Begum = Humayun's wife; Harkha Bai = Akbar's Rajput wife.

questions.text: Which of Aurangzeb's sons rebelled against him during the War of Succession of 1657–58?
options: Dara Shikoh, Shah Shuja, Murad Bakhsh — all three rebelled/competed for the throne
Shah Jahan chose Aurangzeb as successor without conflict
questions.explanation: Shah Jahan (not Aurangzeb's sons — Aurangzeb himself was Shah Jahan's son). Shah Jahan's four sons competed in the War of Succession: Dara Shikoh (eldest, favourite), Shah Shuja (Bengal), Murad Bakhsh (Gujarat), and Aurangzeb (Deccan). All four fought; Aurangzeb won, imprisoned Shah Jahan, and had Dara Shikoh executed.

questions.text: The Peacock Throne (Takht-i-Taus) was built by Shah Jahan but was removed from India by:
questions.explanation: Nadir Shah of Persia invaded India in 1739 CE (under the weak Mughal Emperor Muhammad Shah) and looted Delhi. He took the Peacock Throne (Takht-i-Taus) back to Persia along with the Kohinoor diamond and enormous other wealth. India never recovered these treasures. The Peacock Throne was destroyed in later Persia.

questions.text: The Fatawa-e-Alamgiri, compiled under Aurangzeb's direction, is:
options: A comprehensive compilation of Islamic law (Hanafi fiqh) for governance
A collection of Mughal architectural plans
A record of revenues from Aurangzeb's Deccan campaigns
questions.explanation: Fatawa-e-Alamgiri was compiled by a committee of scholars under Aurangzeb's patronage — a comprehensive codification of Hanafi Islamic jurisprudence (fiqh). It was the largest Islamic legal compilation of its time and was used as a reference for governance of the Mughal Empire.

questions.text: Shah Jahan built the Red Fort (Lal Qila) in Delhi, but his court was housed there only after the capital was shifted from:
questions.explanation: Shah Jahan's initial capital was Agra (which had the Agra Fort). He built Shahjahanabad (Old Delhi) with the Red Fort as its centrepiece (constructed 1638–1648) and shifted his capital to Delhi. The famous saying about paradise on earth ('Agar firdaus bar ru-ye zamin ast, hamin ast') is inscribed in the Diwan-i-Khas of the Red Fort.

questions.text: Jahangir's autobiography 'Tuzuk-i-Jahangiri' (Jahangirnama) is particularly known for its:
options: Vivid descriptions of nature, art, painting, and personal observations
Description of European trade with India
questions.explanation: The Tuzuk-i-Jahangiri (Jahangirnama) is famous for Jahangir's love of nature, art, and painting. He described exotic animals and birds in extraordinary detail (the turkey, the zebra); he assessed paintings by identifying artists from individual faces; he described the Chain of Justice (gold chain with bells hung from the fort). It is one of the most readable Mughal autobiographies.

questions.text: Which Sikh Guru founded the Khalsa (the initiated Sikh community) in 1699 CE?
questions.explanation: Guru Gobind Singh (the 10th and last Sikh Guru) founded the Khalsa on Baisakhi, April 13, 1699 CE at Anandpur Sahib. He instituted the five Ks (Panj Kakars: Kesh, Kara, Kachera, Kanga, Kirpan) and transformed Sikhism into a saint-soldier tradition. After Guru Gobind Singh, the Guru Granth Sahib became the eternal Guru.

questions.text: The Jama Masjid in Delhi, the largest mosque in India, was built by:
questions.explanation: Jama Masjid (Masjid-i-Jahan-Numa — 'Mosque Commanding a View of the World') was built by Shah Jahan (1644–1656 CE). It is the largest mosque in India, accommodating ~25,000 worshippers. Built of red sandstone and white marble with three domes and two 40-metre minarets, located in Old Delhi (Shahjahanabad).

questions.text: Aurangzeb's 25-year Deccan campaign primarily failed to destroy the Maratha power because:
options: Maratha guerrilla warfare in difficult terrain was impossible to permanently suppress
Aurangzeb fell ill in the first year
questions.explanation: The Marathas under Sambhaji, then Rajaram, then Tarabai, used guerrilla warfare (Ganimi Kava) in the Western Ghats terrain to survive Aurangzeb's massive army (500,000 men). Even when Aurangzeb captured individual Maratha leaders (Sambhaji) and forts, the resistance continued. The Deccan campaigns bankrupted the Mughal treasury without achieving permanent suppression.

questions.text: The 'Humayun's Tomb' in Delhi is significant as an architectural precursor because:
options: It is the tallest Mughal monument
It was the first structure to use marble completely
It is the prototype/precursor of the Taj Mahal — first Mughal garden-tomb (charbagh)
It was built by Akbar for his father
questions.explanation: Humayun's Tomb (1565–1572 CE, built by Hamida Banu Begum) is considered the prototype of the Taj Mahal — the first fully developed Mughal garden-tomb (charbagh style). Designed by Persian architect Mirak Mirza Ghiyas, it introduced the double dome, char bagh (four-quadrant garden), and sandstone-marble combination that peaked in the Taj Mahal. UNESCO World Heritage Site.
